summary: Gruber reports a detail buried on page 67 of Apple's Universal Binary Programming Guidelines: Classic (Mac OS 8/9) applications will not be supported under Rosetta on Intel-based Macs. He notes this hasn't been reported elsewhere or officially announced by Apple. While he doesn't find it surprising, he recognizes it as a definitive end to the Classic Mac era. He later confirms Apple has no plans for Classic support on Intel Macs.
key_points: Apple's Universal Binary Programming Guidelines explicitly list Mac OS 8/9 apps among software Rosetta will not translate
Rosetta's limitations also exclude AltiVec code, G4/G5-specific apps, kernel extensions, and certain Java applications
The omission of Classic support on Intel Macs marks the definitive end of the Classic Mac OS era
Apple made no official announcement about dropping Classic — it was buried in developer documentation
money_quotes: I don't think this is surprising, but it's certainly the end of the Classic Mac era.
I have confirmed that Apple has no plans for Classic support on Intel-based Macs.
key_insight: Apple quietly buried the death of Classic Mac OS compatibility in a developer PDF, marking the definitive end of an era without fanfare.
